author: Dr. Vivian Chen (Dermatology) / Dr. Sarah Lo (Dermatopathology)
content:
DERMATOLOGY CONSULT — HOSPITAL DAY 6, WITH AMENDED SKIN BIOPSY PATHOLOGY
Date/Time: 3/20/2027, 3:30 PM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
Referring service: Internal Medicine / Rheumatology
Interpreter: Spanish phone interpreter present.

REASON FOR CONSULT:
Evaluation of scaling dermatosis. Re-biopsy from active 'flaky paint' lesion on abdomen requested by rheumatology to better characterize the cutaneous changes given the differential of SLE/DM cutaneous disease, kwashiorkor-associated dermatosis, or combination.

BRIEF HX:
23-year-old woman with established SLE, now with inflammatory myositis (biopsy 3/19, awaiting final read at that time), severe protein-calorie malnutrition / kwashiorkor pattern, and a diffuse scaling dermatosis that has been progressive over the past 2-3 months. Prior skin biopsy 2/10/2027 (during AMA admission) from a left elbow lesion read as 'interface dermatitis with vacuolar changes... features that could be seen in nutritional dermatosis.'

DERMATOLOGIC EXAMINATION (bedside, full skin survey):
Vitals: stable. Patient cachectic, anasarcic.

Hair: Diffuse non-scarring scalp alopecia, prominent. On close inspection of multiple long strands, clear 'FLAG SIGN' present: alternating bands of normal dark-brown pigmentation and lighter (almost reddish-tan) pigmentation along single hair shafts. This is a classic finding in kwashiorkor / protein-energy malnutrition representing episodes of nutrient deficiency. Hair is also brittle and tugs easily.

Nails: Pale nail beds. Multiple Beau's lines (transverse depressions) on fingernails, particularly the thumb, index, and middle. No nail pitting. No periungual erythema. No nail fold capillary changes visible to gross inspection.

Face: Slight facial fullness from corticosteroid effect overlapping with malnutrition cachexia. No frank malar rash today. NO heliotrope (no purplish discoloration of eyelids). Mild bilateral periorbital edema. Lips dry, mildly cracked. Tongue: small (~3 mm) shallow ulcer; no glossitis.

Trunk and extremities (the area of greatest interest):
- DIFFUSE, well-demarcated to ill-demarcated, dry, pinkish-brown to hyperpigmented, fine to coarsely scaling plaques covering the V-area of chest, upper back, abdomen, and extending to the bilateral upper and lower extremities.
- On the abdomen and thighs: areas show the classic 'FLAKY PAINT' or 'CRAZY PAVEMENT' dermatosis — irregularly shaped, well-demarcated patches of darker, scaly skin alternating with normal-appearing skin, with overlying fine desquamation. In one area of the right lateral abdomen, ~3 × 5 cm patch where the skin appears to be lifting off in coarse flakes.
- Over the dorsum of MCPs and PIPs of both hands: pinker, slightly more raised patches with mild scale. These could represent Gottron-like changes (suggesting DM) versus general dermatosis distribution.
- On the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees: dry, scaly, hyperpigmented plaques. The left elbow site of the prior 2/10 biopsy is healing with visible scar tissue but surrounding skin matches the broader dermatosis pattern.
- No definite discoid lupus lesions (no atrophic scarring discoid plaques).
- No frank vasculitic lesions (no purpura, no ulcerations).
- No livedo reticularis.
- No urticarial vasculitis.
- No Raynaud-related digital changes (no ulceration, no pitting).
- No mucous membrane involvement beyond the oral ulcer noted above.

Genitalia/perineum (with chaperone, brief inspection): No genital ulcers. Diffuse dryness and scaling consistent with overall pattern. No herpetic appearance.

DERMATOLOGIC ASSESSMENT (initial impression):
The cutaneous pattern is COMPLEX with overlapping features suggesting AT LEAST TWO co-existing dermatologic processes:

1. PROTEIN-ENERGY MALNUTRITION / KWASHIORKOR-ASSOCIATED DERMATOSIS:
   - 'Flag sign' hair: highly specific
   - 'Flaky paint' / 'crazy pavement' dermatosis on abdomen and trunk: characteristic
   - Beau's lines: consistent with episodic growth arrest
   - Diffuse skin dryness, scaling: consistent
   - Hypopigmentation areas: consistent
   - Skin fragility / desquamation: consistent

2. CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED CUTANEOUS FINDINGS:
   - Photosensitive distribution on V-area (chest, upper back, extensor surfaces): historically consistent with SLE photo-sensitive eruption
   - Pinker patches over MCP / PIP knuckles: could represent Gottron's-like changes (DM-suggestive)
   - Diffuse non-scarring alopecia: can be seen in SLE
   - Mucosal oral ulcer: SLE criterion

BIOPSY:
A fresh punch biopsy (4 mm) was taken from the right lateral abdomen 'flaky paint' lesion under local 1% lidocaine. Single suture closure. Specimen sent to dermatopathology with explicit request for comprehensive evaluation including: PAS (fungal), Gomori methenamine silver (fungal), Congo Red (amyloid), immunofluorescence (lupus band test), and clinical-pathologic correlation.

---

DERMATOPATHOLOGY FINAL REPORT — finalized 3/22/2027:

SPECIMEN: Punch biopsy, right lateral abdomen 'flaky paint' lesion, 3/20/2027.

MICROSCOPIC EXAMINATION:

EPIDERMIS: 
- Mild flattening with broad attenuation of the epidermis in some areas.
- Hyperkeratosis with overlying parakeratosis in pockets.
- Focal areas of intraepidermal pallor — clear cytoplasmic vacuolation of keratinocytes in the upper spinous and granular layers (classic finding in nutritional dermatosis / kwashiorkor; also can be seen in pellagra and necrolytic migratory erythema patterns).
- Mild basal vacuolar changes at the dermal-epidermal junction in some sections (consistent with interface dermatitis).
- No striking acantholysis.
- No suprabasilar cleft.
- Scattered apoptotic keratinocytes.

DERMIS:
- Sparse to moderate superficial perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate.
- A few histiocytes scattered in the upper dermis.
- Mild solar elastosis given photoexposed location.
- Dermal mucin: NOT INCREASED on alcian blue (argues against pure SLE/DM dermatosis where dermal mucin is often increased).
- No granulomas. No vasculitis. No frank lymphocyte exocytosis.

DERMAL-EPIDERMAL JUNCTION:
- Vacuolar changes present focally but NOT as pronounced as classic SLE interface dermatitis.
- No subepidermal blister formation.

IMMUNOFLUORESCENCE (lupus band test):
- IgG: focal, faint, granular deposition at the DEJ.
- IgM: focal granular deposition.
- IgA: negative.
- C3: focal granular deposition.
- Pattern is overall WEAKLY POSITIVE lupus band test but in a non-classic pattern.

SPECIAL STAINS:
- PAS: no fungal organisms.
- GMS: no fungal organisms.
- Congo Red: no amyloid deposits.

DERMATOPATHOLOGIC DIAGNOSIS:
The biopsy shows features of an INTERFACE/VACUOLAR DERMATITIS with EPIDERMAL PALLOR AND CYTOPLASMIC VACUOLATION of upper-layer keratinocytes. The constellation is most consistent with a MIXED PROCESS:

1. KWASHIORKOR / NUTRITIONAL DERMATOSIS — supported by the cytoplasmic vacuolation pattern in the upper epidermis (NOT typical of SLE-specific interface dermatitis), the relative absence of dermal mucin (typically present in SLE/DM), and the mild perivascular infiltrate.

2. UNDERLYING SLE-ASSOCIATED DERMATITIS — supported by the focal interface vacuolar changes at the DEJ and the weakly positive lupus band test.

This is consistent with the clinical impression of a co-existing CTD-related dermatosis and a SUPERIMPOSED, ADDITIVE nutritional / kwashiorkor dermatosis.

Differential consideration NOT supported:
- No findings to suggest pemphigus or other autoimmune blistering disease.
- No granulomatous process.
- No infection (PAS/GMS negative).
- No amyloidosis.
- No frank dermatomyositis-specific changes (heliotrope changes typically show more prominent vascular involvement; Gottron's typically has more interface dermatitis pattern with mucin).

CLINICAL CORRELATION:
Given the strong clinical signal for kwashiorkor pattern (flag sign hair, severe hypoalbuminemia, anasarca, weight loss), and the biopsy showing features compatible with nutritional dermatosis layered on top of SLE-pattern interface changes, we recommend:

1. PRIORITIZE nutritional intervention as primary cutaneous therapy — many scaling findings will improve with refeeding alone.
2. Continue baseline SLE-directed therapy (HCQ, immunosuppression).
3. Topical skin care: emollients (petroleum-based, non-fragranced), gentle cleansing, sun protection.
4. Avoid topical corticosteroids over large areas given systemic steroids and infection risk.
5. Monitor for healing with refeeding — kwashiorkor dermatosis typically improves substantially within 2-4 weeks of adequate protein/calorie restoration.
6. Re-evaluate at 1 month if cutaneous findings persist after nutritional restoration.

This represents a substantial diagnostic refinement: the prior 2/10/2027 biopsy was equivocal but did note possible nutritional features. Today's biopsy from a more characteristically-affected site provides clearer histopathologic support for a kwashiorkor-pattern dermatosis layered on the underlying CTD picture.

Recommendations communicated directly to rheumatology (Dr. Park) and nutrition (Eun-Ji Lee, RD) at 4 PM. Patient and mother updated through interpreter — explained that her 'skin will get better as her body gets better with food.'
attestation:
Electronically signed by:
Dr. Vivian Chen, MD (Dermatology, Consultant Attending) — procedure and clinical impression
Dr. Sarah Lo, MD (Dermatopathology, Pathologist of Record) — histopathologic interpretation
Date: 3/20/2027 (consult); 3/22/2027 (pathology finalized)
